# Transcrição: 04-Strategies To Reduce Facebook Costs (CPM) - Improve Stability & Profitability 

- **Fonte:** 04-Strategies To Reduce Facebook Costs (CPM) - Improve Stability & Profitability 
- **Duração:** 58:05
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:07]

The topic I want to talk about today is CPM and Facebook costs, because this has been coming up a lot more again like it does all the time. I mean, everyone's always talking about Facebook is expensive. I'm going to bring my cost down, etc. We spoken this week's agency client review meeting, by the way, we have a weekly review of all the accounts together as a team and we brainstorm ideas and come up with solutions. And we noticed a couple of the accounts are still struggling with higher CPMs than we'd like. So conversion rate is good. Average order value is good, for example. And I often say that with CPM, so CPM, first of all, cost per thousand impressions. It's how kind of Facebook are looking at what to charge you, essentially. So if you want to reach a thousand people, sometimes the CPM is low because maybe there's not that much competition, or maybe you're going after people that like to engage or brand awareness, the broader the objective is of what your goal is from Facebook. Generally, the lower the CPM is because it comes down to how big the audiences are, what the competition's like, and also what the value of that one individual, and obviously many in the group, is to you and to Facebook. So if I give you the simplest example, if you are going after a particular user and you are going after the page post engagement objective, the value generally is lower both to you and to Facebook. And there are far more people who are likely to engage in an ad than there are to buy it. Now, because of that and because of the auction dynamics, you pay less for that user. If you're after someone who's recently purchased, has purchased many times, has purchased within your niche, etc., etc., take beauty and hair care, for example. This is a common one where we often see with beauty and hair care, CPMs are generally just high and it can be harder to bring those costs down. There's definitely some ways and I'll show you that shortly. But you know, in the beauty and hair care niche in particular, the CPMs are high because if you think about the audience here, so generalizing, women 25 to 45, let's say, and that's a super competitive niche wherever you go, because everyone wants to target the people that are most likely to buy and generally speaking, that market is generally a higher propensity to buy audience than others. So if you take that, then you layer on, I want people that have bought beauty products or hair care products, etc., then you look at all the competitors in your market, whether it's the US or a European country or worldwide, it doesn't matter what it is. For every you, for every advertiser that wants to access that particular demographic, there's probably like thousands and thousands of competitors out there. So what Facebook have to do is figure out, right, you want access to this demographic. What am I going to charge you? What can I charge you that's going to attract you to spend more with us?

## [03:08 - 06:10]

But at the same time, make sure that we give the user the best experience we can give them as well, because if you followed any of the previous discussions where I talk about the three main players when it comes to Facebook advertising, there's Facebook, there's the user, and then there's you. And ultimately, the main thing that Facebook's looking at is, yes, they want your money as an advertiser, but if they're going to weigh up, do I want this advertiser or do I want this user, they would choose the user 10 out of 10 times, because without users, there is no advertising, there is no Facebook, for example. So if you take that in mind, when Facebook's looking at pricing, it's saying which advertiser can give the user the best experience. It might sound obvious, maybe that's something you've not thought about before, but when you really understand that, that's when you can start to make gains in reducing your costs and by doing so, getting better users from the auction. So, you know, one of the key things about this is when you are looking at your ad campaigns and things are rolling on, if generally the results are good, you know, whatever you're measuring, cost per acquisition, cost per lead, return on ad spend, cost per whatever, registration, when things are going well, often people will neglect looking at some of the other metrics and it's not to say you have to look at absolutely everything all the time, but for example, we were looking at a client ad account today, today, yeah, today, and we noticed that the CPMs were creeping up, although ROAS was really good, so this is an e-com store, for example, we're looking at the RAS is 350% and it's, you know, way over the target, they're spending, I don't know, six, eight K a day and you can look at that and say things are great, let's just continue piling more money, but because I've been doing this for so long, I know the things that could go wrong and with the creeping CPMs, that means gradually I'm paying more for my acquisition costs, more for my clicks, etc. If for any reason that conversion rate drops, the costs are going to rise and my return on ad spend is going to get more expensive, so even though CPMs are costs are not a major concern on that account right now, we're still keeping an eye, we're looking at right, what can we do to analyze why the CPMs are creeping up and what do we do about it? In some cases, there's nothing you can do because CPM is often driven by the auction. Take for example, when we come into, and this is the particular time when CPMs are impacted most, is the last two weeks of March. This has happened since I can remember, going back six, seven years. The last two weeks of March, just a universal rule that seems to happen, is that the costs rise. So whatever niche you're in, generally the costs go up. So what's actually happening is a lot of the big advertisers out there, they often, and I'm talking about, they've got millions and millions and millions of, or even tens of millions of budget to spend, and these are kind of global brands and things like that.

## [06:10 - 09:12]

When it comes to setting their budgets for the year, often the next year is based on how much you spent the previous year, plus what you're asking for, etc. Sometimes what happens is, let's say you have a $10 million budget for the year, the 12 months from April to March. If you don't spend all of that, often the finance team will come back and say, well, you asked for $10 million, you've only spent $8 million, why are you asking for $12 million for the following year? Because you failed to spend that. So what sometimes happens is agencies and in-house teams will panic spend towards the end of the financial year, sometimes the end of the quarter, and it seems to hit March the most, because a lot of companies have their financial years, April to May. So what happens is the costs go up just because there's so much more competition coming in from people with bigger budgets that don't generally care as much as you do about ROAS, CPA, CPL, and all those kind of things. They just want to get that budget out there because the next year depends on it. So when you're playing against that, that becomes a lot more difficult. Now, in that kind of situation, that's competition-based. You can't really do much about that. There are some advanced strategies where you can go into manual bidding, or you could just pull your budget back. Like, bear in mind, Facebook is not a guaranteed return. There are going to be occasions where sometimes your results are not performing as well as they need to, and you need to take a step back, reduce your budgets, analyze what's going on. But for example, take the last two weeks of March, when those costs do come up, if you're not going to go aggressive and start manual bidding and start pushing, sometimes you have to take a step back and realize that, and this is something I want you to take away as well. In these kind of situations, making wholesale changes can have a massive negative impact. So if you're in this phase, let's say end of March, end of April, May, June, etc. So at the end of the quarter, if you start to see these CPMs creeping up, keep that in the back of your mind that it could be something way out of your control that's actually causing your costs to go up. And the worst thing you can do is to change your strategy. Change your strategy. So you're like, you know, my results are all over the place. I'm going to try new audiences. I'm going to scrap the strategy that was working two weeks ago. I'm going to try something new. I've seen something in a Facebook group. I've seen these hacks. I'm going to multiply and duplicate and that will bite you in the butt because what often happens, and I see this so many times, is people will steer away from what was working because the costs have gone up and they're like, clearly I'm doing something wrong. But it could be that it's out of your control. So just bear that in mind before you start thinking about wholesale changes. Is the cost that I'm seeing go up due to external factors? And you know, that's why I run these private groups and these private sessions as I do in the training so that you can ask. I in the agency have access to some big numbers and we get to see across the board what's happening as well. So, you know, for a lot of you as you're starting off on your spends or, you know, managing fewer accounts, maybe you're not seeing the global picture.

So most people will say, this is your star audience and that's all you should optimize.

## [15:17 - 18:18]

The problem is that's also the most expensive audience, generally. So what drives your decision making to use that audience or not? So once you understand what kind of makes up that the kind of process, it becomes easier to understand what to do. So one of the things that I often talk about is the bare the algorithm. So first of all, the bid makes a difference in your cpm. If you use auto bid, you're letting Facebook make that decision based on signals you're giving, and I'll talk about that signal. If you're using manual bid, you are overriding that. Now when you go into manual bidding and it is a bit more advanced, when you understand how to manipulate that, there is a general rule that the higher you bid, the better you can access the more expensive auctions and the better users, but it's not always the case. Then you have the estimated action rate. This is Facebook saying, right, this advertiser is after the purchase event. What's the chance of that actually happening? How do they measure that? Generally your conversion rate percent. So Facebook's looking at that and saying, right, you're optimizing for purchase. What's the chance of that purchase happening? Now if you've got no data, so you've, you know, so new account, new website, you've never run this before, then Facebook doesn't have anything to run by and generally it will give you better users in the auction because it has to start collecting that data. Over time, it will track your site conversion rate and feed that into the algorithm. So this is why conversion rate is important. Then you've got user value. This is about the engagement and how users are finding your your ad experience and your landing page experience, your funnel experience, etc. And Facebook places a lot of value on this in the auction. So what is user value? One of the measures, click through rate, because if there's a really good click through rate, it could be that your ad is engaging and relevant. They look at things like site speed. So how fast is your website loading? That plays a big difference and Google were the first ones that made, that created the research on this to show site speed and advertising and ROI and CPA. Those things are all aligned. So the faster your page loads, generally the better the conversion rate. Now those have done lots of studies since Google were also the first ones. ## [18:18 - 21:23]

that makes sense because if the user has a good experience, the ad is good, the ad engagement is good, the complaints are low, the conversion is high, the page loads fast, etc. etc. Equals, the user is happy, equals, the advertiser gets good performance, equals everyone's happy. So things like, click through rate, site speed, but there's also other signals that Facebook gives you as well. So you've got the quality engagement scores as well within Facebook as well. So previously this was the relevance score and now Facebook has broken that down into three different measures, which looks at your essentially looking at your targeting, looking at your ad engagement, and also your landing page experience. So this is what Facebook break that down into. If your targeting is good, your ad experience is good, the landing page experience is good, that plays a big part into reducing your cpms because you're giving the user a good experience. So this is why I think that unless you're seriously taking your lander and your funnel into account when you're running campaigns, you're missing out on a huge part of the equation of getting the low cpms and getting the best options in the best using the options. So there is a correlation between getting the best users and also the cpm score. It's not always that the cpm is cheapest for the best users. Sometimes it's more expensive, but maybe the conversion rate goes up as well. So that's something to bear in mind. And then you've got things like the negative signals at the ad level. So negative ad signals. So for example, when you have an ad, you can choose to stop seeing ads by this advertiser. You can also choose to report a complaint. Like this is a spam, this is harassment, this is this, this is that. What Facebook are trying to do is ask the user specifically and say are you happy with this ad? And if that user comes back with a negative signal, that has a massive negative impact on that ad's performance. So sometimes you'll see that the costs for specific ads go up, but actually maybe the ad set overall is okay and the other ads are okay. If I see that, it gives me an indication that maybe there's a problem at the ad level. So, you know, that's one of the signals to look for. But the other one, which I think goes neglected is also comments. Because when you get negative comments in your ads, they can stack up the more and more people that add negative sentiment. So Facebook are looking at the comments as well as the engagement overall. That can also have an impact of higher CPM because Facebook are now punishing you. This is Facebook's way of saying, hey guys, there's a problem here. You need to sort this out because the user's not happy. And if the user's not happy, hey, we're not happy either. So we're going to charge you more. So there's, on the one side, you know, if I go back to the previously when I talked about external factors and a few things you can't control,

you shouldn't understand that there's so much more in your control when you understand what Facebook are after. Because the thing is, when you've got a high click-through rate, but a low conversion rate, that's telling Facebook that there's a disconnect here. So don't, you know, I don't want your takeaway to be the highest click-through rate is the best click-through rate. Now you want to balance between a good click-through rate and a good conversion rate. And I'm sure the question will be, well, what is a good click-through rate? What is a good conversion rate? Generally, you know, this is just a universal thing and it will kind of depend across niche and vertical. Click-through rates between 1 and 3%. For example, generally okay. On my conversion rate, this is dependent on your average order value and where the product sits in terms of how easy it is to explain and understand. So, you know, I use Maslow's hierarchy of needs to understand where that product sits in terms of that person's needs. I use what I call the Core 4 and I look at kind of breaking out the key reasons why someone will want to buy. So does it solve a problem? Does it create a new opportunity? Those come into constructing my ads because I want to combine a good click-through rate with a good conversion rate. Conversion rate is not just what you do on your website. So what I would be looking at is whilst I'm trying to work on the ads to create a good click-through rate, I'm also measuring and modesting my conversion rate as well. That's exactly what Facebook are looking at and I know that. So that's why I'm trying to balance those things out. So I'm giving Facebook the best positive signals. And by the way, on most of my accounts, I rarely do manual bidding. The reason for that is when you've got a really good conversion rate, when you've got a really good click-through rate and all of these, you know, you're giving Facebook all the right signals about what's happening, Facebook automatically bid you at the highest level to get the best user. Because it's in Facebook's interest now because what you're saying is I'm giving the user the best experience I can. And therefore, you know, with the funnel performing, Facebook looks at that and says, I'm going to give you more traffic. I'm actually going to bid higher for you because you're doing our job for us. You're giving the user a great experience.

## [30:29 - 33:34]

and things like that, and each of these strategies are as you need them. So, they're not designed as you need to go through all of them sequentially. When you understand the different types of elements that go into cost control, and the first part is just understanding that, for example, one of them is page engagement. Now, with the Facebook page, this is something which I spoke to the Facebook auction insights team back in 2018 to understand organic reach of pages and the link between Facebook pages and ad accounts. One of the things that they told me at the time was one of the, so it wasn't quite on record, but this is through testing, we've found this as well. The more engaged people are on your Facebook page, the lower your CPMs become as well. What's the reason for that? We can thank low-quality dropshippers, low-quality affiliates, low-quality brands that don't care about the user experience for this, because what happens is, and this is a typical example, if you're a dropshipper and you're only here for the money, you're not really caring about your page. So, you're not going to update your page, you're not going to put a nice banner on, you're not going to worry about engagement and things like that. Facebook know that, so they've built that into their model to say, if this advertiser does not care about their page, well, guess what? We're going to increase the cost. So, viral page engagement reach test, what we're trying to do here is, when we're in a position where we need to improve the engagement at a page level, we start testing different page posts to see which ones are the most engaging, promote them more and create more content like that on our page as well. That allows you to also have ideas for your ad campaigns. So, that's a whole strategy in its own page as well. looking at the ad level and loading up the three engagement columns and looking at the scores. Now, what Facebook do is they judge you on who you're bidding against, so the other competitors and how they are performing. Now, one of the things that I wanted to point out on this is if you have, for example, let me just bring back my drawing utensil. So, if I just move this to the side, so one important thing I want to get across from this is a common question I have on, well, why should we optimize for different parts of the funnel? Now, let's say you've got advertiser A and you've got advertiser B. So, Facebook sends 100 clicks into each. So, Facebook are, you know, you create your clicks from the ads and 100 clicks come through. Let's say, for example, advertiser A converts five people from every 100 click, advertiser B, one person. So, essentially, that's your conversion rate, 5% and 1%. When Facebook look at this, what are they seeing? They're seeing that advertiser A is actually converting more people than advertiser B. So, there must be something about advertiser A that's performing better. What they don't care about or maybe don't look at as much is what the average order value is. So, for example, let's say you have a $50 AOV here and you have a $350 AOV here, you might look at that and say, actually, 1% conversion rate is okay. It's an expensive product. For this one, you're like, I'm really happy, 5% conversion rate. If you look at that, your return on ad spend could be lower for your $50 product. Maybe it's okay. Your return on ad spend could be really wild and, you know, it's great. For example, it could cost you $50 to acquire that $50 customer. Facebook don't really care if you're making money or not. Obviously, they look at ROAS and they're trying to give you better users. But in my experience and running this for many years, conversion rate has a bigger impact on what Facebook see on your site than average order value. So, obviously, if you're doing webinars and leads and things like that, then generally, conversion rate is what you're looking at. That conversion rate, Facebook is looking at based on what you're optimizing for. So, if you're optimizing for conversion and purchases, then Facebook's looking at that purchase conversion rate. If you're optimizing for initiate checkout, if you're optimizing for ad to cart, that's the conversion rate that Facebook is looking at. So, for example, if for every 100 people that click through here, let's say 30 people ad to cart. So, 30% go to ad to cart. Maybe here, also 30 people ad to cart. Now, all of a sudden, your conversion rates are actually similar. So, you've got 30% conversion rate here. What happens here? Facebook says, actually, both advertisers are performing really well and I'll give them more users like that because they're given a good experience. Your CPM generally drops and you

## [36:36 - 39:37]

get access to better users. One argument is, if you're optimizing for ad to cart, you get lower quality users. Generally, what this means is they might not be identified as purchases. Maybe they haven't purchased in 60 or 90 days. Maybe they've just been added adding to cart more than anything. It does not mean they are not purchases. So, for example, when we do higher ticket promotions, we often test purchase, but then we end up going with ad to cart on issue. Check out, because this gives us top of funnel stability. What that means is when you're running your campaigns, $1,000 a day, $2,000, $5,000, $10,000 at the top of the funnel, because we can get stability at the top of the funnel and because our retargeting campaigns are working so well, we actually get better performance. And what happens now is retargeting is optimized for purchase. And in that case, we could be converting at 5% or more. Facebook sees our retargeting campaign and says, hey, that's really good. You're getting a really high conversion rate. We'll give you more users. The top of the funnel, we convert it at 30% and Facebook is like, hey, you're performing really well. We'll give you some better users. So, when you understand why these strategies come into play, when it comes to stabilizing your ad account and giving you good performance, not to say all the time, it has to be ATC, but certainly the higher the average order value, the longer it takes someone to generally convert. So, for example, even we've got an Apparel company at the moment, the average order value is about $100. We know whatever return on ad spend number we get today, in two weeks time, it'll double because whatever clicks we generate today, they will trickle into sales in five, seven, 10 days because that's the consideration process that people go through. You can't force that. You can try and run even more retargeting ads, but that's through 18 months, two years of running ads for this particular brand. We know that there is that conversion path. So, we do top of funnel is to bring them in and then we work on optimizing and converting where we can through retargeting and trying to get them through to our email list. But that has given us on that particular account, which I think it's like a million dollars a month in revenue on that particular account, the CPM in the US for females, I think 25 to 50 is about $10 or $12. And it's been consistent like that for years. That means that we are doing many of the things right. I wouldn't say everything, there's still a few opportunities, but that means we're doing so much right to tell Facebook that we care about the user. So, going back to our initial drawing, we've worked really hard on giving Facebook the signals. And that means that Facebook give us the best users at the best cost. And that's hard to compete against when you're a competitor, because when you start to enter the market that we're in right now, you're not going to get $10, $12 for that audience. It takes time. And it means going through what we've done and set up and step by step,

bringing the right things in. Then you've got auction insights and campaign objectives and different strategies. I talk about average order value optimisation, because that gives you a bit of space to give you some profitability so you can scale up. ## [42:42 - 45:43]

as well. So, Simon, is CPM the main metric to focus on or do you combine CPM and CPC? Simon, CPM is not a main metric at all. It's like the fuel gauge or the temperature gauge on your car. When you're driving, you don't look at that to dictate what you're actually doing. If your fuel is low, then you know you need to take an action. If your fuel is high, you just kind of keep an eye on it. That's how I see CPM. So, when I'm running ad accounts, I'm not constantly tracking CPM. I might have a look once a week just to see what the trends are like. Generally, I don't look at CPC in too much detail unless my cost per acquisition is not performing because cost per click and conversion rate are the things that impact cost per acquisition. So, if I know my CPA is not in a good place, then I'll look at CPC and stuff like that. So, I don't want you to take away that now you need to monitor CPM every day and make decisions based on that. That's not the case. Like I mentioned previously, if your CPM is not in a good place, but overall your ROAS or your CPA is in a good place, it's just something you need to keep an eye on on the side. And I think it's just getting the balance right between what you're doing on both those pages as well. What does good page engagement look like? So, post per week, engagement per post? I think I shared something many months ago on this. So, when you look at your number of fans, generally the most you can reach organically, and this is just a generalization, is 10% of your fans. So, if you've got 10,000 fans, as a general rule, you can't access more than a thousand people just the way the organic algorithm works. And it could be less in many cases as well. So, we just did this analysis for one of our clients and they got a UK and a US Facebook page for their two separate businesses. And the UK page averages 30% organic reach. So, on average, 30% of the total number of fans are getting, are responding and engaging with our page post. And then it's 5% for the US. What's the difference? In the UK, for the last probably 18 months, they're posted every single day. In the US, I believe they have 6,000 fans and they barely post. So, that's the simplest example of post per week engagement and stuff like that. And they're only reaching 5% of those 6,000. So, 300 people, that's not a lot. So, in order to increase that, and we've just taken on the

## [45:43 - 48:44]

US responsibility, we are going to go through these exact same steps to get that engagement up. Now, 6,000 fans is not that much. So, we're looking to increase the number of likes on the page. And the best way we've found to do that is when you're running ads or you're running content on your page, click on the little like icon on the post. It will pop up the people that have liked the post or the ad. And it will show you who's actually liked your page already. And all you need to do is hit the invite link to invite them to like your page. There are external tools to do this. Bear in mind, they're all against policy. So, I would not recommend using them. I know a lot of people do, just better in mind that it's against policy. So, if you do use an external tool for this, and there's Chrome plugins, make sure it's as non-robotic as possible. So, robotic would be, it goes through, it clicks it, and it just, you know, every half a second says invite, invite, invite, invite. That's a guaranteed profile ban or going into Facebook jail, which means you can't like or comment or share on a post. But the way we do it, for example, is we have our customer service rep once a week, just do this process, but vary the time between invites as well. If you're using link click through rate, often it will include links within the actual instant collection as well, which don't count. Like if someone clicks a link and opens up and expands the, the products that they're looking at, that can count as a link click, but it doesn't count as a click into your actual website. ## [48:44 - 51:48]

rate in prospecting and retargeting. Does it have an impact on prospecting? If you have better numbers in the retargeting, so does it have an impact on a prospecting? I think overall it can have an impact at the account level and you know, your pixels collecting data. But the thing I'm looking at is the signals I'm giving back to Facebook at the ad set and the ad level. So obviously if you're running prospecting, then the ad is different at the top of the funnel than if you're running ads for your retargeting. So that's what I'm looking at. Can I give Facebook positive signals? Yeah, every interaction. So when that adds in that news feed, can I give Facebook the positive signals that my ad is good, my click through rates good, my negative experience is low, etc, etc. Then once they come through the site, you know, like I said, Facebook looking at page speed, funnel performance, our people, like for example, one of the big negative signals that Facebook pick up is if someone clicks an ad and they land on your site, but within 30 seconds, they bounce back and go back to Facebook, essentially that's a bounce. So, you know, talk about bounce rate in Google Analytics. Facebook see that as a bounce as well. If they don't take an action, so the action could be that they add to car, they sign up to your email, and whatever it is, maybe a view another page, that's a positive action. So if you, for example, and this is why I've been telling people for many years, don't delay your pixel fire, because how does Facebook know whether someone's engaging on your page is because the pixel exists. That pixel is collecting all the HTML on your website. And when someone clicks from Facebook to your site, the pixel is the beacon. It's the thing that's telling Facebook what's actually happening on the site. So I know there's been some talk over many years about people saying, oh, you should delay the pixel fire and, you know, so that your retargeting audiences have the best people. Well, actually, if you delay your pixel fire, what does Facebook see? It sees nothing. So if it sees nothing, Facebook's assuming your page is still loading. So, you know, you might, I've had lots of discussions about this to explain to people why it's wrong. But if you delay your pixel firing, let's say 10 seconds, as far as Facebook's concerned, your page took 10 seconds to load. That's not good. So, you know, that's just something to aside things to bear in mind as well. How does CPM compare for that company in the UK and US? you just can't get the best price. That's an easy, not an easy, but that's a clear path that we can follow. For lead gen ads, I'm looking at cost per click and conversion rate as to what my cost per lead is. They're the two metrics that influence that. For example, when I run lead forms, generally conversion could be 20, 30 percent, and the cost per click is generally low. However, and this is probably eight out of 10 times, when I run lead form, lead gen versus website lead gen, although it costs me more on the website to capture the lead, the conversion, that the quality is generally better. Why is that? There's a few different factors. Number one, when you do a lead gen on Facebook, defaults by pulling in their email that they use for Facebook. Now, me, for example, I signed up with Facebook in 2007 and it's an email account I don't really use. So if I was to see a lead ad and I clicked it and I just use the default Facebook email, I wasn't really thinking about changing it, then that email that you send me for that amazing offer or funnel or anything like that, probably going to end up in my spam email address and I'm probably not going to see it. And that's something I've found through testing as well. Whereas when you take them through a website experience and you talk a bit more, you've got some more real estate, and you say, enter your best email address and make sure your white list does and things like that, you can communicate that better. So there's just something as a generalization that we've found that running website leads generates better quality leads as well. So it's

something for you to test as well. So, Jay, I'll come back to your question. Calibration testing, I would use CBO and I just let the budget run until I've hit my maximum number of impressions.
